segments.title: HMS Rutherford’s American-built origin
segments.summary: Meet HMS Rutherford, a Captain-class frigate built in the United States as a Buckley-class destroyer escort and employed by the Royal Navy from 1943 to 1945.
segments.body: Rutherford was K558, one of the American-built escorts that entered Royal Navy service as Captain-class frigates. The short introduction establishes both sides of her identity: an American Buckley-class destroyer-escort design and a British wartime coastal-forces command ship.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies K558 as a Captain-class frigate built as a Buckley-class destroyer escort and gives her Royal Navy service dates.

segments.title: An escort designed for reach rather than speed
segments.summary: Rutherford’s dimensions, boiler plant, 24-knot top speed, and 5,500-nautical-mile range show why an escort’s endurance could matter more than high speed.
segments.body: The video gives Rutherford a displacement of roughly 1,400 tons and a compact 93-metre hull, powered by two Foster Wheeler water-tube boilers. Her reported 24-knot maximum was modest, but the important operational figure is the approximately 5,500-nautical-mile range at 15 knots. It is a useful reminder that escorts were built to stay on station and cover long routes, not merely to chase the fastest surface target.

evidence.note: The transcript lists the frigate’s displacement, dimensions, boiler plant, speed, and endurance.

segments.title: Anti-aircraft and anti-submarine weapons for coastal work
segments.summary: Rutherford combined three-inch and Bofors guns with a growing Oerlikon battery, Hedgehog, and depth charges for the changing threats of the Channel war.
segments.body: Rutherford carried three-inch guns and a twin 40 mm Bofors mount, with her number of 20 mm Oerlikons increasing as D-Day approached. The video pairs this close-range anti-aircraft armament with a Mark 10 Hedgehog mortar and depth charges. Together, the weapons show an escort expected to face both aircraft and submarines while working with coastal forces.

evidence.note: The transcript lists the gun armament, the enlarged 20 mm battery near D-Day, Hedgehog, and depth charges.

segments.title: A radar-equipped command ship for motor torpedo boats
segments.summary: Radar, sonar, and direction-finding equipment made Rutherford an information hub that could direct smaller motor torpedo boats during coastal operations.
segments.body: Rutherford’s Type 144 sonar, radar sets, and medium- and high-frequency direction-finding gear mattered as much as her guns. Dr. Clarke describes the command frigate as a sensor node: it collected information and passed it to smaller motor torpedo boats. The same explanation also shows why a destroyer could sometimes take over the job, since it might bring comparable sensors with greater overall capability.

evidence.note: The transcript lists Rutherford’s sensors and explains her information-sharing command role alongside motor torpedo boats and occasional destroyer flotilla leaders.
